Sammaan Capital Growth Plan: Diversified NBFC + Massive Scale-Up by FY29

• 📈 AUM Ambition: Targets Top 3 NBFC position with best-in-class RoA & RoE by FY29

• 🔄 Business Shift: Transition from pure housing finance → diversified lending (MSME, personal, gold loans)

• 🌐 Pan-India Expansion: Branch network to scale from 200+ → 1,500+

• 👥 Customer Growth: Targeting ~50 million customers vs 1.4M currently

• 🏦 Product Expansion: Loan offerings to expand from 4 → 15+ products

• 🧠 AI & Digital Push: Leveraging IHC backing for AI-led lending & analytics

• 💪 Execution Strength: Focus on risk management, governance & experienced management team

• 👨‍💼 Hiring Scale-Up: Workforce to grow from 4,000 → 10,000+

🟢 Impact: Positive
Aggressive scale-up + diversification + strong promoter backing → high growth visibility & potential re-rating

GEM ENVIRO: ACQUISITION TIMELINE EXTENDED, STRATEGY INTACT

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• Timeline extended for acquisition of up to 26% stake in Solluz Energy  

DETAILS  
• Original deadline: March 31, 2026  
• Revised deadline: June 30, 2026  
• Reason: Operational delays  
• Board approval: December 26, 2025  

STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE  
• Entry into renewable energy space remains on track  
• No change in deal structure or investment intent  
• Provides clarity on execution timeline

IMPACT

HALDER VENTURE: CREDIT RATING UPGRADE SIGNALS IMPROVING PROFILE

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• CRISIL upgrades long-term rating to BBB-/Stable (from BB+)  
• Short-term rating assigned at CRISIL A3  

RATING DETAILS  
• Long-term: BBB- / Stable (Investment grade entry)  
• Short-term: A3  
• Upgrade reflects improved transparency & cooperation  

CREDIT STRENGTHS  
• Established market position with diversified product portfolio  
• Strong promoter experience (60+ years)  
• Moderate financial profile:  
  – Net worth: ₹153 Cr  
  – Interest coverage: >2.5x  

KEY RISKS  
• Large capex (~₹155 Cr Haldia project, FY27 commissioning)  
• Low operating margins (~2.2%)  
• High working capital cycle (~190 days)  
• Exposure to raw material price volatility  

SIGNIFICANCE  
• Transition to investment grade improves credibility  
• Better access to funding and potentially lower borrowing costs  
• Signals improving governance and financial discipline  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Rating upgrade strengthens credit profile, though execution and margin risks remain key monitorables

MIDDLE EAST: GULF NATIONS’ HARDENING STANCE ON IRAN CONFLICT

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• Gulf nations reportedly push for stronger action against Iran  
• Signals rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East  

KEY DEVELOPMENTS  
• United Arab Emirates reportedly urging Donald Trump to consider a ground invasion of Iran  
• Kuwait and Bahrain also said to support this stance  
• Closed-door discussions involving Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait, and Bahrain  

STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE  
• Indicates shift towards more aggressive regional posture  
• Suggests opposition to diplomatic resolution without structural change in Iran  
• Reflects coordinated Gulf alignment on Iran policy 
RISK IMPLICATIONS  
• Increased probability of military escalation in the region  
• Potential disruption to global oil markets and supply chains  
• Heightened geopolitical uncertainty for global investors  

IMPACT  
• Negative – Rising tensions increase global risk premium and could trigger volatility across energy and financial markets

NTPC LIMITED: SOLAR CAPACITY EXPANSION STRENGTHENS GREEN ENERGY PORTFOLIO

• Khavda-I Solar PV Project: 78.02 MW added (part of 1255 MW)  
• Khavda-II Solar PV Project: 90 MW added (part of 1200 MW)

CAPACITY UPDATE

STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE  
• Accelerates renewable energy capacity expansion  
• Strengthens NTPC’s transition towards clean energy  
• Supports India’s long-term decarbonization goals  

BUSINESS IMPACT  
• Enhances renewable share in overall generation mix  
• Improves long-term sustainability profile  
• Positions NTPC as a key player in large-scale solar deployment  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Steady renewable capacity addition reinforces growth visibility and strengthens NTPC’s green energy positioning

RBI: CALIBRATED EASING ON BROKER FUNDING & MARKET NORMS

CORE HIGHLIGHT  
• RBI eases earlier restrictions on bank funding to brokers  
• Shifts stance from “no funding” → “fully secured funding”  

KEY POLICY CHANGES  
• Banks allowed to fund broker proprietary trades  
• Strict condition: 100% cash / cash-equivalent collateral required  
• Defers new capital market exposure norms: April 1 → July 1  

MARKET STRUCTURE IMPACT  
• Improves liquidity in capital markets  
• Addresses operational concerns of brokers & banks  
• Balances risk control with market functioning  

ADDITIONAL RELAXATIONS  
• Market makers: Collateral usage restrictions eased  
• Clarifications issued on lending norms  

ACQUISITION FINANCE UPDATES  
• Definition expanded to include mergers & amalgamations  
• Allowed only for control of non-financial companies  
• Permitted for subsidiaries (including overseas)  
• Refinancing allowed post acquisition completion  

STRATEGIC SIGNIFICANCE  
• More pragmatic regulatory stance  
• Supports deal-making and capital market activity  
• Ensures risk containment via strict collateral norms  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Enhances liquidity and flexibility while maintaining strong risk safeguards

MID & SMALL CAPS: STRUCTURAL RE-RATING vs CYCLICAL OUTPERFORMANCE

CORE OBSERVATION  
• Mid & Small caps outperforming Nifty even during stress phases  
• Rare historically → typically they underperform in crises  
• Outperformance sustained across 1Y / 2Y / trailing periods  

WHAT’S DIFFERENT THIS TIME  
• Balance sheets:  
  – Lower leverage vs pre-COVID  
  – Reduced interest burden  
• Cash flows:  
  – Better working capital discipline  
  – Higher FCF conversion  
• Profitability:  
  – Operating leverage post demand recovery  
• Corporate governance:  
  – Cleaner businesses vs earlier cycles  

STRUCTURAL DRIVERS  
• China+1 + manufacturing shift → benefits mid-cap exporters  
• PLI schemes → capex cycle revival in mid-sized companies  
• Formalisation of economy → market share gains from unorganised  
• Domestic demand resilience (India-specific growth story)  

WHY THEY OUTPERFORMED EVEN IN “STRESS”  
• This is NOT a balance-sheet crisis (like 2008 / 2013 / 2018 IL&FS)  
• Liquidity still reasonably strong in the system  
• Earnings downgrades limited vs large caps (banks/commodities earlier cycles)  
• Retail + SIP flows structurally supporting broader markets  

VALUATION REALITY CHECK  
• Re-rating justified partly by:  
  – Better quality  
  – Higher growth visibility  
• But:  
  – Many pockets already pricing perfection  
  – Dispersion very high (winners vs junk)  

WHERE YOU ARE RIGHT  
• Earnings growth differential likely higher vs large caps  
• True multi-baggers historically emerge from this segment  
• Quality today is far superior vs pre-COVID  

WHERE CAUTION IS NEEDED  
• Mean reversion still applies (even in structural stories)  
• Liquidity cycles can reverse faster than fundamentals  
• Not all mid/small caps = quality (selection risk very high)  
• Valuation corrections may not be deep like past crashes, but drawdowns (20–40%) are still normal  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Structural bull case intact (India growth + corporate health)  
• But returns will be:  
  – More stock-specific (not index-wide)  
  – More volatile than large caps  

WHAT TO FOCUS ON (CRITICAL)  
• Earnings consistency (not just growth)  
• Cash flow > reported profits  
• Capital allocation discipline  
• Avoid leverage + story-driven narratives  

BOTTOM LINE  
• You’re likely in a structural re-rating phase, not just a cyclical rally  
• Biggest winners will come from mid/small caps — but only high-quality, scalable businesses  
• Expect volatility, not a straight line up

20 sectors in India where TAM (Total Addressable Market) is EXPLODING 

Not narratives. Not hype. This is real market expansion happening right now.
From EV ecosystem → Defence electronics → Diagnostics → Agri tech
Every category below is seeing 2–5x growth potential in the next few years

Most people focus on “stocks”
Smart investors track categories first, because when TAM expands:
1. Even average companies can grow

The real edge?
These sectors are still Under-owned and Under-researched.

How to use this:
Pick 2–3 sectors you understand

The next big wealth creation won’t come from crowded trades.
It’ll come from Emerging sectors + early positioning

reports.text:
Union Minister Ashwini Vaishnav announced the approval of India’s first rare earth permanent magnet (REPM) manufacturing unit.

Lohum Cleantech

Putting up India’s First Rare Earth Permanent Magnet manufacturing facility.

The company counts investors like Madhu Kela led Singularity, Growth I9 Opportunity, Baring Private Equity, Asiana Fund, Cactus Ventures.

Lohum Cleantech is primarily known for battery recycling and critical materials processing. This project extends their capabilities into the full rare earth magnet value chain, supporting EVs, renewables, electronics, defence, and other strategic sectors.

The co is putting up 700 Crore into the facility. This is highlighted as a first-of-its-kind project using fully indigenous technology and intellectual property (IP), as stated by Union Minister Ashwini Vishnaw. It marks the first time India will produce sintered rare earth magnets (like NdFeB) domestically at this scale from oxides.

This single facility is expected to help meet around 25% of India’s domestic demand for rare earth magnets initially.

Risk assessment thesis for Mold-Tek Packaging
RBI FOREX TIGHTENING

TD POWER SYSTEMS: NICHE CUSTOM GENERATOR PLAYER WITH STRONG MOAT IN MID-SIZE SEGMENT

CORE THESIS  
• Engineered-to-order AC generator specialist (1 MW – 200/250 MVA)  
• Positioned in under-served mid-size/custom segment  
• Strong export-led model with global presence (100+ countries)  
• Beneficiary of industrial capex + energy transition + data center demand  

NICHE POSITIONING  
• Focus: 1–50/60 MW customized applications (core strength)  
• Bridges gap between:  
  – Small gensets (commoditized)  
  – Large utility-scale OEMs (GE/Siemens focus area)  
• High customization → better pricing power & client stickiness  

STRUCTURAL EDGE (MOAT)  
• Engineering-led, customized solutions (not commoditized)  
• Strong OEM partnerships (Voith, Siemens licensing)  
• Flexible supplier to turbine OEMs & EPC players  
• High switching cost due to design integration  

GROWTH DRIVERS  
• Industrial capex revival (steel, cement, sugar, paper)  
• Renewable + co-generation demand  
• Data center boom → gas-based captive power demand  
• Export markets (diversified geography reduces cyclicality)  

PRODUCT & APPLICATION DIVERSITY  
• Prime mover compatibility:  
  – Steam, gas, hydro, wind, diesel engines  
• Applications:  
  – Geothermal, solar thermal  
  – Oil & gas, marine, mobile power  
  – Industrial captive power (ethanol, steel, etc.)  
• Adjacent offerings: motors, traction motors, spares & refurbishment  

BUSINESS MODEL STRENGTH  
• Asset-light vs large EPC players  
• Higher margins vs commoditized generator players  
• Aftermarket (spares/refurbishment) adds annuity element  
• Export-led mix supports margin stability  

KEY RISKS  
• Cyclicality in industrial capex  
• Dependence on global demand (exports)  
• Competition from global OEMs if they enter mid-size segment aggressively  
• Technology dependency (licensed products)  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• “Sweet spot” positioning between commoditized and mega-scale players  
• Customization + export mix = sustainable competitive advantage  
• Plays on multiple themes: industrial revival, energy transition, data centers  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Strong niche positioning with durable moat and multi-sector demand tailwinds

reports.text:
US, Japan, S Korea Encourage Manufacturers To Consider India

 Reports

Focus On Legacy Semiconductor Nodes In India

**VIKRAM SOLAR: BACKWARD INTEGRATION LED SOLAR PLAY WITH CYCLICAL CAPEX → FCF INFLECTION**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Transition: Module player → Integrated module + cell manufacturer  
• Capacity: Modules 9.5GW → 15.5GW; Cells 12GW (from FY27)  
• Earnings CAGR: Revenue/EBITDA/PAT ~68%/81%/110% (FY25–28)  
• Margin rebuild post FY27 via DCR mix + in-house cells  

**GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Strong solar demand: ~200GW additions (FY25–30)  
• Policy tailwinds: BCD, ALMM, DCR, PLI push localisation  
• DCR mix: ~36% volume → ~47% revenue (FY28)  
• Module volumes: 3.2GW → 9.3GW (FY26–28 ramp-up)  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE**  
• Backward integration into cells improves realisations  
• Positioned in DCR (domestic content) premium segment  
• Beneficiary of India’s localisation push vs China dependence  

**FINANCIAL PROFILE**  
• Revenue: ₹34bn → ₹162bn (FY25–28E)  
• EBITDA: ₹4.9bn → ₹29.3bn  
• PAT: ₹1.4bn → ₹13bn  
• RoE: ~16.6% → ~29% | RoIC: ~14% → ~19%  
• EBITDA/Wp: ₹2.8 → ₹1.9 → ₹3.2 (dip then recovery)  

**CAPEX & FCF INFLECTION**  
• Heavy capex: ~₹78bn (FY26–28), peak in FY27  
• FCF negative during expansion phase  
• Strong FCF generation expected from FY28 onward  

**VALUATION**  
• TP: ₹290 (~52% upside)  
• Valuation: 8x FY28E EPS (~50% discount to peers)  
• Discount factors: Late cell entry + lower integration + execution risk  
• Re-rating dependent on execution & margin delivery  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Execution risk in 12GW cell commissioning  
• Module oversupply → pricing pressure  
• Input volatility (poly, glass, silver, aluminium)  
• Policy changes (BCD/ALMM/DCR)  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Growth, Execution-Heavy) – Strong earnings inflection story, but success hinges on timely integration and margin recovery

reports.text:
**SHRIRAM FINANCE: HIGH-YIELD NBFC WITH ROA EXPANSION STORY**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Diversified, secured retail NBFC with strong used-vehicle & MSME franchise  
• AUM CAGR ~18% (FY26–28E) with PV, MSME & gold as key drivers  
• RoA expansion: ~3.1% → ~3.5–3.8% led by NIM improvement  
• Liability transformation post MUFG → structural CoF reduction  

**GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• AUM: ₹3.0 tn → ₹4.2 tn  
• PV & MSME: ~20–25% CAGR  
• Gold loans scaling rapidly (strong rural demand)  
• New vehicle share rising (cross-sell + lifecycle play)  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE**  
• Leadership in used vehicle financing (~97% used assets)  
• Strong rural/semi-urban underwriting franchise  
• Secured granular book (~90%+)  
• Deep OEM/customer ecosystem  

**FINANCIAL PROFILE**  
• Yield: ~16.5%+ | NIM: ~9–9.7%  
• RoA: ~3.5–3.8% | RoE: ~14%  
• Credit cost: trending <2%  
• Strong provisioning buffer (ECL ~5.9%)  

**VALUATION**  
• TP: ₹1,150 (~27% upside)  
• P/E: 17.5x → 13.3x (FY26–28E)  
• P/B: 2.7x → 1.7x  
• Re-rating driven by RoA expansion + lower CoF  

**KEY RISKS**  
• CV/MSME cycle slowdown  
• Crude/geopolitical shocks impacting borrowers  
• MSME/CE credit cost volatility  
• Rising competition in used vehicle financing

reports.text:
**MSUMI: OPERATING LEVERAGE + CONTENT GROWTH + EV OPTIONALITY STORY**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Business model: High operating leverage + rising content per vehicle  
• Revenue CAGR: ~13% (FY26–28) driven by PV, 2W and EV growth  
• Earnings CAGR: ~27% with strong margin expansion  
• Greenfield ramp-up: Key driver of profitability and RoCE improvement  

**KEY GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Greenfield utilization: ~45% → 65–70% (FY27) → further upside in FY28  
• Content growth:  
  – PV: ~1.7x increase (EV + premiumisation)  
  – 2W: ~3x increase with EV transition  
• Segment growth: PV & 2W outperform CV and agri/off-road  
• Strong OEM pipeline visibility due to dominant market position  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE (MOAT)**  
• Market leadership: >55% PV harness share, >25% in 2W  
• High customer stickiness with OEM integration  
• Sumitomo partnership → access to advanced systems (ECUs, junction boxes, EV solutions)  
• 30 manufacturing facilities with fungible capacity (flexibility across programs)  

**VALUE CREATION MODEL**  
• Operating leverage from utilization ramp-up  
• Content per vehicle expansion → structural revenue growth  
• Mix improvement (premium vehicles + EVs) boosts margins  
• Automation + disciplined capex enhances RoIC  

**OPTIONALITY (HIDDEN VALUE)**  
• EV transition: Multi-year structural content increase  
• Premiumisation (shift to UVs): ~1.5x content uplift in PVs  
• Advanced electronics integration → move up value chain (beyond wiring harness)  

**FINANCIAL TRAJECTORY**  
• Revenue: ₹111.7bn → ₹143.0bn (FY26–28)  
• EBITDA: ₹10.6bn → ₹16.1bn (~20–26% growth)  
• EBITDA margin: 9.5% → 11.3%  
• PAT: ₹6.1bn → ₹9.9bn  
• EPS growth: 23.7% (FY27E), 31.0% (FY28E)  
• RoE: 41–50% | RoCE: 49–60% (best-in-class returns)  

**BUSINESS MIX & OPERATING LEVERAGE**  
• Revenue mix:  
  – PV ~62% (core driver)  
  – 2W ~13–14% (fastest growth)  
  – CV ~9–10%, Agri ~6%, Others ~8–9%  
• Key plants: Navagam (Gujarat), Pune, Kharkhoda → utilization ramp critical  
• Capex: ~₹2bn annually with strong FCF (₹5.1bn → ₹9.3bn)  
• High payout ratio (~85%) supported by strong cash flows  

**COST STRUCTURE & RISKS (INPUT SIDE)**  
• Gross margin stable: ~33–34%  
• Cost mix: Employee ~17–18%, Other expenses ~6–7%  
• Copper exposure: ~10% of revenue  
  – Pass-through: 60–70% (3 months), balance (6 months lag)  
• Imports: ~27–28% of RM (limited near-term disruption risk)  

**VALUATION**  
• P/E: 41x / 33x / 25x (FY26E / FY27E / FY28E)  
• EV/EBITDA: 23.9x → 15.6x (compression with growth)  
• Target Price: ₹60 (40x FY28E EPS)  
• Premium justified by:  
  – High visibility growth  
  – Strong RoE/RoCE  
  – Structural EV tailwinds  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Weak domestic auto demand  
• Competitive intensity (global players like Aptiv, Yazaki)  
• Commodity volatility (copper) with lagged pass-through  
• Currency fluctuations impacting imports  

**WHERE ASSUMPTIONS ARE AGGRESSIVE**  
• Sustained double-digit volume growth across segments  
• Smooth Greenfield ramp without delays  
• Margin expansion despite RM volatility  
• Continued premium valuation multiples  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Not a commoditized harness player → evolving into system solutions provider  
• EV + premiumisation = structural, not cyclical, growth  
• High operating leverage + content growth creates strong earnings compounding  

**WHAT TO TRACK (CRITICAL)**  
• Greenfield utilization trajectory (45% → 70%+)  
• EBITDA margin expansion vs guidance  
• EV penetration and content per vehicle trend  
• Copper price movement and pass-through efficiency  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Quality Compounder) – Strong structural tailwinds, high return ratios, but valuation leaves limited margin for error

TRIVENI ENGINEERING: E20 PUSH POSITIVE, BUT NEAR-TERM SUPPLY TIGHTNESS

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• E20 rollout (20% blending) structurally boosts ethanol demand  
• Industry already has 60–70% excess ethanol capacity  
• No immediate capex expansion planned (capacity sufficient)  

BUSINESS DYNAMICS (SUGAR + ETHANOL)  
• Sugar & ethanol both performing well (linked economics)  
• Ethanol blending not at risk → grain-based supply flexible  
• Diversion to ethanol lower vs earlier estimates  

NEAR-TERM SUPPLY OUTLOOK  
• Lower cane availability → reduced sugar & ethanol output  
• Crushing season ended earlier than expected  
• Tight supply → supportive for sugar prices  

PRICE DRIVERS  
• Sugar prices stable to slightly higher (+1–1.5% in Q4)  
• Global factors:  
  – High crude → more ethanol diversion globally  
  – Brazil shift impacts global sugar supply  
• Weak monsoon → bullish for sugar prices  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• E20 ensures long-term demand visibility  
• Future upside: potential move toward E27–E30 / E100  
• Ethanol-based applications (e.g., cooking fuel) = optionality  

SEGMENTAL IMPACT  
• Core (Sugar + Ethanol): Stable to positive outlook  
• Engineering: Impacted by Middle East slowdown  

RISKS  
• Cane availability & monsoon dependency  
• Policy changes in ethanol blending  
• Global crude & Brazil dynamics  

IMPACT  
• Positive (Structural) – Ethanol demand strong; near-term supply tightness supports sugar prices

**LLOYDS METALS: MINING → STEEL INTEGRATION STORY WITH HIGH OPERATING LEVERAGE**

**CORE THESIS**  
• Transition: Merchant miner → Integrated steel player  
• Revenue: ₹67K Cr → ₹224K Cr (FY25–FY28, ~3.3x growth)  
• EBITDA: ₹17K Cr → ₹78K Cr (massive operating leverage)  
• Margin expansion: ~29% → ~35% (mix + cost advantage)  

**KEY GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Surjagarh mine ramp-up: 10 → 26 MTPA (core engine)  
• Pellet capacity: 4 → 12 MTPA (value addition)  
• Wire rod mill (FY27): 1.2 MTPA high-margin steel  
• Future HRC plant (FY29): next growth leg  

**STRUCTURAL EDGE (MOAT)**  
• Captive iron ore cost: ~₹1,933/t vs ₹5,500–6,000/t market  
• Logistics edge: slurry pipeline (₹850–1,250/t savings)  
• Captive power → energy stability  
• Integrated model → higher EBITDA/t  

**VALUE CREATION MODEL**  
• Ore → Pellets → Steel = margin ladder  
• EBITDA/t expands significantly across value chain  
• Mix improvement (not just price cycle) drives earnings  

**OPTIONALITY (HIDDEN VALUE)**  
• Thriveni (79.8% stake):  
  – MDO business → annuity-like cash flows  
  – Value contribution: ~₹209/share  
• Copper JV (DRC):  
  – Not valued in SOTP (pure upside optionality)  

**FINANCIAL TRAJECTORY**  
• Revenue CAGR: ~33% (FY24–29)  
• EBITDA margin: ~26% → ~35%  
• PAT: ₹12K Cr → ₹64K Cr  
• ROE moderates but remains strong (~27–35%)  
• Net debt peak manageable (~1x EBITDA target)  

**VALUATION (SOTP)**  
• Standalone: ₹1,522/sh (12x FY28 EV/EBITDA)  
• Thriveni: ₹209/sh  
• Total TP: ₹1,730 (≈32% upside)  
• Valuation compresses sharply with earnings growth  

**KEY RISKS**  
• Single-asset dependency (Surjagarh mine)  
• Execution risk (multiple projects simultaneously)  
• High capex cycle → balance sheet stress  
• Commodity cycle (steel/pellet spreads)  
• Regulatory & ESG risks (mining + DRC exposure)  

**WHERE ASSUMPTIONS ARE AGGRESSIVE**  
• Sustained ~35% EBITDA margins  
• Smooth execution of all capacity ramps  
• Strong steel spreads without major downcycle  
• High ROE despite heavy capex  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Not a pure mining play → emerging integrated steel story  
• Cost leadership + integration = structural advantage  
• Thriveni adds hidden value & cash flow stability  

**WHAT TO TRACK (CRITICAL)**  
• Ore ramp-up: 20 → 26 MTPA execution  
• Pellet + steel spreads (EBITDA/t)  
• Wire rod commissioning timeline  
• Net debt vs EBITDA discipline  

**IMPACT**  
• Positive (High Conviction but High Risk) – Strong structural story, but execution & cycle sensitivity remain key

SECTOR VIEW: JYOTIVARDHAN JAIPURIA – POSITIONING ACROSS MARKETS

CAPITAL MARKET PLAYS (BROKERS / AMCs / DEPOSITORIES)  
• View: Avoid / Not Attractive

  – Earnings normalization after strong years  
  – Valuations still not compelling despite correction  
• Key Risk: Lower volumes → earnings downgrade cycle  

BANKING SECTOR  
• View: Positive / Preferred Sector  
• Rationale:  
  – Attractive valuations (P/B comfort)  
  – NIM recovery expected in FY27  
  – Stable asset quality  
• Preference: PSU + select private banks  

MACRO CONTEXT (RBI)  
• Rate cuts unlikely near term  
• Focus shifted to currency stability (INR defense)  
• Implication: Banks benefit from stability vs rate-sensitive sectors  

METALS  
• View: Neutral → Buy on Corrections  
• Strategy:  
  – Not chasing at current levels  
  – Accumulate on sharp dips  
• Trigger: Geopolitical supply disruptions (e.g., aluminium)  

IT SECTOR  
• View: Structurally Cautious  
• Concerns:  
  – Slower growth outlook (3–5 years)  
  – AI disruption risk  
• Strategy:  
  – Selective buying in niche/specialty IT (after 40–50% corrections)  
  – No broad-based bullish stance  

PHARMA  
• View: Constructive (Bottom-Up)  
• Rationale:  
  – Stock-specific opportunities (not purely macro driven)  
  – Relatively insulated from global conflicts  
• Key segments:  
  – US generics (after correction)  
  – CDMO (long-term story)  
  – Domestic pharma  

OVERALL STRATEGY  
• Prefer: Banks + Pharma  
• Tactical: Metals on dips  
• Selective: Niche IT  
• Avoid/Underweight: Capital market plays  

IMPACT  
• Positive tilt toward defensives & value sectors; cautious on cyclicals linked to market activity

reports.text:
Buying the Future, Not Nostalgia

​The attached article serves as a sobering "reality check" for investors who equate a company's past glory with its future returns. While giants like Asian Paints, HUL, and TCS remain fundamentally strong businesses, the data from 2019 to 2026 highlights a critical lesson: valuation acts as gravity. When quality stocks are bid up to "priced-for-perfection" levels—sometimes exceeding 70x or 80x earnings—even solid profit growth can result in "lost decades" or paltry returns as multiples inevitably compress.

​The shift from the 2009–2019 "golden era" to the current slowdown proves that a great company can be a poor investment if the entry price ignores a margin of safety. As interest rates normalized and liquidity tightened, the market stopped paying for "nostalgia" and began demanding tangible earnings acceleration to justify high premiums. For the modern investor, the takeaway is clear: dominance in the market does not guarantee dominance in your portfolio if you overpay for the privilege of owning it.

Input Costs & Weak Rupee Drive Electronics Price Hikes

Price Surge in Entry-Level Segment
- Entry-level TVs, smartphones, ACs seeing hikes after years of decline
- Prices approaching 6–8 year peak levels

Key Cost Drivers
- Higher input costs (chips, metals) + rupee depreciation
- War-led inflation adding upward pressure

Specific Price Movements
- Entry-level 5G phones: ₹10K → ₹13–14K (may hit ₹20K)
- 32-inch TVs: ₹6,500 → ₹8,500 year-over-year
- AC prices also registering sharp increases

Industry Response
- Brands absorbing some costs but planning 5–12% further hikes
- Shift from discounts/cashbacks → higher effective prices

Demand Impact
- Consumers delaying purchases or opting for refurbished devices
- Retailers seeking GST cut on smartphones (18% → 5%)

Iran Strikes Disrupt Gulf Aluminium Supply

Production Impact
- Iranian strikes hit key aluminium plants in UAE & Bahrain
- Infrastructure damage could delay production recovery
- Middle East produces ~9% of global aluminium output

Logistics Bottlenecks
- Strait of Hormuz disruption risks export constraints
- Supply chain issues straining global inventories

Price Pressure
- Aluminium prices already rising on supply fears
- Further spikes expected from tighter regional supply
- Stockpiles covering near-term industrial demand

Corporate Response
- Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) assessing damage
- Operations status remains uncertain

Market Implications
- Geopolitical risk creating aluminium supply shock
- Higher metal prices adding inflationary pressure
- Global economy faces commodity cost escalation

BANKING SECTOR: RBI CAPS FOREX POSITIONS – MTM RISK & VOLATILITY CONTROL

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• RBI caps net open forex position (NOP-INR) at $100 Mn (from Apr 10)  
• First explicit cap since 2011 (earlier bank-driven limits)  
• Aim: curb rupee volatility amid global uncertainty  

IMMEDIATE IMPACT  
• Banks may unwind ~$30–40 Bn positions  
• Potential MTM losses due to forced squaring-off  
• Likely short-term rupee appreciation  

EARNINGS IMPACT  
• Risk of FY26 earnings hit from MTM losses  
• Trading income may decline due to lower flexibility  
• Volatility in treasury performance  

STRATEGIC VIEW (RBI INTENT)  
• Reduce speculative positioning in forex markets  
• Strengthen currency stability amid:  
  – Dollar strength  
  – Geopolitical tensions  
• Improve risk discipline in banking system  

SECTOR CONCERNS  
• Sudden implementation → limited transition time  
• Banks seeking relaxation for existing positions  
• Reduced ability to benefit from FX trading opportunities  

OUTLOOK  
• Short term: volatility + earnings pressure  
• Medium term: more stable FX environment  
• Key variables: crude prices, capital flows, geopolitics  

IMPACT  
• Neutral to Negative – Near-term MTM losses & lower trading gains, but improves systemic stability

ANTI-DUMPING DUTIES: PROTECTION VS COST PRESSURE – DOUBLE-EDGED IMPACT

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• DGTR increasing anti-dumping actions across raw materials & intermediates  
• India among largest users of such trade measures globally  
• Focus largely on protecting domestic producers  

IMPACT ON INDUSTRIES  
• Higher input costs passed down value chain  
• Margin pressure on downstream sectors:  
  – Textiles  
  – Chemicals  
  – Engineering  
• Fragmented consumer industries lack representation  

STRUCTURAL CONCERN  
• Current framework ignores downstream impact  
• Risk of policy bias toward organized producers  
• Can lead to cost inflation across industries  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Anti-dumping = double-edged sword:  
  – Protects domestic manufacturing  
  – Hurts competitiveness of downstream users  
• Risk of shifting from fair trade → protectionism  

WAY FORWARD (SUGGESTIONS)  
• Mandatory downstream impact assessment before duties  
• Independent evaluation mechanism  
• Balanced policy between producers & consumers  

IMPACT  
• Neutral to Negative – Supports upstream players but pressures downstream margins & competitiveness

FUJIYAMA: ALMM-II POLICY TAILWIND – DOMESTIC SOLAR PLAY SCALING UP

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• ALMM-II mandate (from June) → domestic solar cell sourcing push  
• Strong demand tailwind for local manufacturers  
• Current capacity: ~1.5 GW (panels + ecosystem)  

CAPEX & EXPANSION  
• 1 GW solar cell plant already commissioned (~₹300 Cr)  
• Potential expansion to 1–2 GW based on demand visibility  
• Ongoing capacity addition in Madhya Pradesh  

BUSINESS STRATEGY  
• Integrated model: panels + inverters + batteries  
• Increasing backward integration (75% domestic sourcing already)  
• Future plan: lithium cell manufacturing in 2–3 years  

DEMAND DRIVERS  
• Rooftop solar boom (Tier II/III cities)  
• Policy push for domestic manufacturing (ALMM-II)  
• Import substitution opportunity  

RISKS  
• Domestic solar cell availability & pricing constraints  
• Continued reliance on imports in short term  
• Policy execution & demand variability  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Clear beneficiary of India’s solar localization push  
• Moving toward fully integrated renewable manufacturing player  
• Capacity expansion aligned with structural demand  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Policy tailwinds + capacity expansion support strong growth opportunity

HFCL: FIBRE EXPANSION + DEFENCE FORAY – BUILDING DUAL GROWTH ENGINE

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• Planned capex: ₹800–900 Cr (fibre + defence + preform)  
• Order book: ₹10,000+ Cr (strong visibility)  
• Defence subsidiary order book: ₹1,700 Cr  

CAPEX & EXPANSION  
• Fibre capacity: ~30 mn fkm → 40+ mn fkm  
• Preform investment: ₹580 Cr (backward integration)  
• Additional ₹300–400 Cr in fibre & defence  
• Tech collaboration with IIT Delhi  

BUSINESS STRATEGY  
• Dual-engine growth:  
  – Telecom (optical fibre, cables)  
  – Defence & aerospace (new vertical)  
• Backward integration → reduces import dependency  
• Focus on high-growth segments: data centres & hyperscalers  

BUSINESS IMPACT  
• Strong demand tailwinds in fibre (data + digital infra)  
• Defence diversification → improves revenue mix & stability  
• Export exposure (>80%) → currency tailwind  

RISKS  
• Raw material cost inflation (West Asia impact)  
• Telecom equipment segment facing pressure  
• Execution risk in defence scaling  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Transitioning from telecom supplier → integrated tech + defence player  
• Capex-led growth with long-term structural tailwinds  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Strong order book + capacity expansion + defence diversification support long-term growth

- Current scheme lacks storage for agriculture pump solarisation

Storage Proposal Details

- Enables supply during peak evening/night demand hours
- Improves efficiency, reliability, and solar power utilisation

Key Stakeholders Involved
- Discussions between Power Ministry, MNRE, and Finance Ministry
- Tackles grid management challenges from variable solar output

Strategic Energy Goals
- PM-KUSUM pillars: decentralised solar plants, standalone pumps, grid-connected pumps
- Reduces diesel dependence for agriculture
- Enhances farmer energy security through clean energy transition

Global Energy Shocks Accelerate India Electrification

Import Vulnerability Exposed
- $140B oil & gas import bill highlights exposure to global shocks
- Russia-Ukraine and Iran conflicts disrupting supply chains
- Electrification emerging as urgent strategy to cut import dependence

Accelerated Power Demand
- Power demand to grow at 6.4% CAGR through 2025–2030
- New drivers: EVs, data centres, appliances, cooling demand
- Data centres to contribute 2–3% of power demand by 2030 (4% by 2035)

Balanced Energy Mix
- Reliable base load requires continued coal & nuclear role
- Strong renewables push + 10–15 GW annual battery storage addition
- Hybrid solutions (solar + storage/pumped hydro) to address intermittency

Infrastructure Challenges
- Grid expansion, transmission capacity remain critical bottlenecks
- Capacity addition pace must accelerate to meet demand trajectory

Strategic Priorities
- Balance energy security, cost efficiency, and sustainability goals
- Shift from gradual transition to urgent electrification mandate

Top Government Sources to NDTV Profit On ATF

There will be an impact on ATF prices from next month due to higher crude oil prices

Trying to help all stakeholders ie. Airlines, Passengers, Airports

In discussions with all government stakeholders

Assessing ways to minimise impact of high crude oil prices

Pankaj Tibrewal of Ikigai Asset on Channel Checks 

On-ground checks amid ongoing global tensions — what we are seeing so far:

Over the past few days, our team connected with multiple companies across sectors to understand how the current situation is unfolding at the ground level. A few key observations:

1. Demand remains resilient across sector till now 

Encouragingly, there is no visible demand destruction so far. Most of sectors in-fact said demand has been quite decent till now. For eg: most auto ancillary companies indicated that OEM schedules for even April–May remain healthy. However, given recent price increases across sector, some moderation in demand over the coming months cannot be ruled out.

2.Margins holding up (for now)

Margins remain stable as companies are still consuming lower-cost inventory.Price hikes have been meaningful across sectors, and most corporates are proactively securing raw material supplies to ensure business continuity. However if situation persist then margin headwind could be see in 1Q and part could be negated by higher top-line growth. 

3. Shift towards shorter-term contracts:

There is a clear preference for shorter-duration contracts—fortnightly or monthly—on both raw materials and finished goods. With uncertainty around commodity prices, companies are avoiding long-term commitments at elevated levels.

4. Unorganised sector under pressure, market share shift to leaders across sectors 

Smaller players are facing challenges due to working capital constraints and raw material volatility. This is leading to a gradual shift in market share towards stronger, well-capitalised leaders.

5. Labour availability becoming a constraint for MSMEs:

Several companies highlighted emerging labour challenges. In certain pockets, workers are returning to their villages due to issues like LPG availability, upcoming elections, and lingering fears of a Covid-like disruption. This is beginning to impact operations across small scale industries.

6. Logistics disruptions increasing lead times:

Logistics is becoming a bottleneck, with fuel availability at highways turning inconsistent. Delivery timelines, which were earlier around 4 days, are now stretching to 7–8 days in many cases. Freight rates from China to India has gone by 2x. 

7. Chemicals sector seeing tailwinds:

The chemical sector continues to witness strong demand. Supply pressures from China have eased due to logistics disruptions and currency movements, supporting better spreads. However, raw material availability remains a constraint.

8. Petrochemical chain seeing availability constraints:

The broader petrochemical value chain is witnessing both availability constraints and pricing volatility, creating uncertainty across multiple downstream industries.

9. Innovation and adaptability on display

Encouragingly, companies are responding with agility. For instance, some AC/EMS players that were dependent on gas cylinders for welding and paint shops are now shifting to alternative processes like oxy-acetylene, helping mitigate supply disruptions and maintain continuity.

10. Preparing for structurally higher energy costs

Many corporates believe that even if the war subsides, oil prices may remain elevated. Countries are likely to build strategic reserves after this energy shock, keeping demand firm. As a result, the entire oil value chain could see sustained inflation—creating top-line tailwinds for several sectors, even as cost pressures persist.

11.Inflation: not all bad for earnings

A moderate level of inflation is often supportive for corporate earnings and nominal GDP growth, as it drives higher realisations and revenue growth. While there could be some pressure on margins and currency in the near term, the overall setup remains constructive.

Periods like these often accelerate market share gains for stronger players, while also setting the stage for nominal growth tailwinds in an inflationary environment.

**No rare earths = no missiles = no war**

Even before the war started, RE stockpiles were low, and China, the biggest exporter, restricted exports on key military-linked REs

- Neodymium: 90% comes from China
- Gallium: 95% of supplies come from China
- Graphite: 80% of supplies come from China
- Tungsten: 80% of supplies come from China

One could say no China = no war.

This puts the US in a rather uncomfortable situation in which their military relies on the final endboss they plan to defeat

NBFC SECTOR: NOMURA – FUEL SHOCK MAY TRIGGER CREDIT RISK CYCLE

KEY TAKEAWAYS  
• Rising fuel costs disrupting MSME-heavy industrial belts (Gujarat, TN, Maharashtra)  
• Early signs of stress in MSME lending segment  
• Risk of spillover into vehicle finance & broader credit cycle  

STRESS BUILD-UP (CORE ISSUE)  
• MSME loans (~24% mix) under pressure  
• Fuel shortages + higher costs → cashflow disruption  
• Likely impact:  
  – Higher credit costs  
  – Slower loan disbursals  

SECOND-ORDER IMPACT  
• Lower economic activity → reduced transport demand  
• Vehicle finance segment next at risk:  
  – Fewer trips → weaker borrower cashflows  
  – Rising delinquencies possible  

MARGINS & FUNDING PRESSURE  
• Cost of funds rising (G-sec yields ~6.5%+)  
• Margin pressure across NBFCs  
• Lending growth may turn cautious  

SECTOR DIVERGENCE  
• Better placed:  
  – Housing finance  
  – Diversified NBFCs  
  – Gold loan players (defensive, secured)  

• Higher risk:  
  – MSME-focused lenders  
  – CV/vehicle finance-heavy NBFCs  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Stage 1: MSME stress emerging  
• Stage 2: Vehicle finance stress may follow  
• Early signs of credit cycle turning due to fuel shock  

IMPACT  
• Near-term: Negative for MSME & vehicle finance NBFCs  
• Relative positive: Housing & secured lending players remain resilient

ASIA MARKETS: NOMURA FLAGS CAUTION – INDIA FACES VALUATION & MACRO RISKS

KEY DRIVERS  
• Iran conflict enters 5th week → no normalization in Hormuz energy flows  
• Oil supply uncertainty remains elevated  
• US–Iran talks ongoing, but no clear de-escalation  

INDIA VIEW (RELATIVELY VULNERABLE)  
• High valuations vs peers  
• Earnings risk amid global slowdown  
• Twin deficit concerns (fiscal + current account)  
• Higher oil sensitivity vs other Asian markets  

REGIONAL CONTEXT  
• Cautious on Asia ex-Japan equities overall  
• Indonesia & Philippines also flagged, but relatively lower risk vs India  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Prolonged high crude = key macro overhang  
• Global risk-off sentiment may lead to FII outflows  
• India premium valuations leave limited margin of safety  

IMPACT  
• Negative – Oil uncertainty + valuation risk could pressure markets in near term

**BANKING & FX: JEFFERIES – RBI NOP CAP MAY TRIGGER MTM LOSSES**

**KEY DEVELOPMENT**  
• RBI caps Net Open Position (NOP) in onshore FX at $100mn  
• Earlier: Board-driven limits (~25% of capital)  
• Surprise move tightening forex exposure norms  

**RATIONALE (WHY RBI ACTED)**  
• Sharp INR depreciation  
• Widening gap between:  
  – Offshore (NDF) market  
  – Onshore FX market  
• Aim: Stabilize INR & curb speculative positioning  

**IMPACT ON BANKS**  
• Forced unwinding of large FX positions by April 10  
• Estimated positions: $30–40bn across large & foreign banks  
• Likely MTM losses in 4QFY26 due to:  
  – Rapid position unwinding  
  – Adverse currency movements  

**SECTOR IMPLICATIONS**  
• Near-term earnings pressure for banks  
• Treasury income volatility expected  
• Large banks & foreign banks most impacted  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Positive for INR stability (macro control)  
• Negative for bank profitability (short term)  
• Regulatory tightening indicates rising FX risk concern  

**IMPACT**  
• Short-term: Negative (MTM losses, treasury hit)  
• Medium-term: Stabilizing INR may reduce systemic risk

Management meet takeaways,

Domestic demand momentum remains healthy, especially in the 350cc segment, where demand currently outstrips supply.

• ₹10/litre excise duty cut offers some relief, but not enough to offset high crude

**INDIA ENERGY: GOLDMAN SACHS VIEW – POLICY SHIFT & MARGIN PRESSURES**

**KEY POLICY ACTIONS**  
• Excise duty cut by ₹10/litre on petrol & diesel  
• Windfall tax reinstated on diesel & ATF exports  
• Government balancing inflation control vs fiscal revenue  

**CRUDE OIL CONTEXT (CORE PRESSURE)**  
• OMC breakeven crude: ~$78/barrel  
• Current crude: ~$122/barrel  
• Implies: Significant margin stress across downstream players  

**OMCs (OIL MARKETING COMPANIES) VIEW**  
• IOC: Least favourable risk-reward among OMCs  
• HPCL & BPCL: Rated Neutral  
• Key concern:  
  – High crude vs capped retail pricing  
  – Margin compression risk  

**REFINING & EXPORT DYNAMICS**  
• Windfall tax impacts export realizations  
• RIL refining margins likely impacted  
• Policy overhang reduces upside from high global cracks  

**GLOBAL PRODUCT CRACKS (SUPPORTIVE BUT LIMITED BENEFIT)**  
• Diesel cracks: ~$75/barrel (elevated)  
• Jet fuel cracks: ~$46/barrel  
• However: Export taxes dilute benefit for refiners  

**RIL OUTLOOK**  
• FY27 refining EBITDA estimate: ~$12/barrel  
• Moderation expected due to:  
  – Export tax impact  
  – Volatile crude-product spreads  

**STRATEGIC INSIGHT**  
• High crude = structural negative for OMCs  
• Government intervention caps profitability upside  
• Refiners face policy risk despite strong global spreads  

**RISKS**  
• Sustained high crude prices  
• Further policy intervention (taxes, price controls)  
• Volatility in global demand & refining margins  

**IMPACT**  
• Negative bias for OMCs due to margin pressure & policy overhang  
• Mixed for refiners like RIL – strong cracks offset by taxation risk

reports.text:
CITI on OMCs

Fuel tax changes announced by the gov’t today provide meaningful relief to the OMCs while capping refining upsides for RIL

Following are changes that have been announced

1) Cut in excise duty on petrol & diesel by Rs10/ltr (not passed through to retail prices, lowering marketing losses for the OMCs)

2) Imposition of export duty of Rs21.5/ltr on diesel and Rs29.5/ltr on jet fuel (limits refining upsides for RIL to the extent of domestic sales to OMCs, assuming its SEZ refinery continues to be exempt – await clarity on this).

Notably, the absence of a windfall tax on upstream marks a departure from historical policy, potentially allowing ONGC to fully benefit from higher crude prices

BANK ; RBI Tightens Dollar Positions; Banks May Face ₹4,000 Cr MTM Hit

• ⚖️ New Rule: RBI caps NOP-INR (net $ exposure) at $100 Mn per bank

• ⏳ Deadline: Banks must reduce positions by April 10

• 💰 Huge Exposure: Banks reportedly holding ~$40 Bn+ long USD positions

reports.text:
ONGC: INDIA’S $20 BILLION DEEP-SEA ENERGY BET UNDER “SAMUDRA MANTHAN”  

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• ONGC committing ~$20B toward deep-sea oil & gas exploration  
• Strategic shift: energy security becomes national priority  
• Focus on offshore basins, especially Andaman region  

INDIA’S ENERGY DEPENDENCE (CORE PROBLEM)  
• ~88% crude oil imports  
• ~60% LPG imports  
• Significant reliance on imported natural gas  
• High vulnerability to geopolitical shocks (West Asia, Hormuz)  

STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE  
• Reduce import dependency  
• Build long-term energy independence  
• Hedge against global supply disruptions  

SAMUDRA MANTHAN VISION  
• Large-scale offshore hydrocarbon exploration program  
• Targeting untapped deep-water reserves  
• Integrating energy, logistics & blue economy strategy  

OFFSHORE FOCUS (KEY OPPORTUNITY)  
• Andaman basin: high-potential hydrocarbon reserves  
• Comparable geology to Indonesia (~700K barrels/day output)  
• Potential upside: up to ~2M barrels/day (long term)  

TECHNOLOGY & EXECUTION  
• Deployment of advanced deep-sea rigs (15–20 storey structures)  
• Use of:  
  – Floating rigs  
  – Robotic submersibles  
  – Underwater drones  
• Global partners shortlisted: BP, ExxonMobil, TotalEnergies, Petrobras  
• Aggressive timeline: infrastructure mobilization within ~80 days  

ONSHORE PARALLEL EXPLORATION  
• Ganga basin (Ballia, Samastipur, Prayagraj)  
• Drilling depth up to ~3,000 meters  
• Limited scale vs offshore opportunity  

ENERGY LOGISTICS STRATEGY  
• ₹6 lakh Cr annual spend on energy transportation  
• Push for domestic gas carriers (Cochin Shipyard & others)  
• Goal: reduce import logistics cost + strengthen maritime capability  

INFRA & BLUE ECONOMY INTEGRATION  
• Andaman & Nicobar development:  
  – Transshipment ports  
  – Expanded runways  
  – Urban infrastructure  
• Integration with:  
  – Trade routes  
  – Energy transport  
  – Rare earth exploration  

STRATEGIC INSIGHT  
• Shift from “energy importer” → “resource developer” mindset  
• Offshore exploration = multi-decade structural play  
• Combines energy security + geopolitics + industrial policy  

RISKS  
• Exploration uncertainty (reserves may not scale as expected)  
• High capex intensity & long gestation period  
• Execution complexity in deep-sea environments  
• Dependence on global technology partners  

IMPACT  
• Positive (Long-term) – transformational move toward energy security, with potential to significantly reduce import dependence and strengthen India’s strategic positioning

SEMICONDUCTOR PACKAGING & SILICON PHOTONICS: THE REAL AI BOTTLENECK  

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• AI performance bottleneck shifting from compute → data movement  
• Packaging & interconnects emerging as key performance drivers  
• Silicon photonics likely next major breakthrough in AI infrastructure  

CORE PROBLEM (AI INFRA LIMITATION)  
• AI workloads need massive parallel compute + ultra-fast communication  
• GPUs scaling rapidly, but data transfer becoming bottleneck  
• Latency, bandwidth & power efficiency now critical constraints  

ADVANCED PACKAGING SHIFT  
• Technologies: 2.5D/3D packaging, chiplets, fan-out packaging  
• Enables higher compute density & better power efficiency  
• Industry shift:  
  – Earlier: Transistor scaling drove performance  
  – Now: Packaging + architecture driving performance  

INTERCONNECT BOTTLENECK  
• Copper-based interconnect limitations:  
  – High power consumption  
  – Heat generation  
  – Bandwidth constraints  
• Even advanced packaging cannot fully solve data transfer challenges  

SILICON PHOTONICS (NEXT FRONTIER)  
• Uses light instead of electrons for data transfer  
• Key benefits:  
  – 10–100x higher bandwidth  
  – Lower power consumption  
  – Scalable for hyperscale data centers  
• Applications: GPU-to-GPU, server-to-server, AI cluster connectivity  

CRITICAL CHALLENGE: PHOTONICS PACKAGING  
• Integration of optics + electronics is highly complex  
• Requires micron-level precision in alignment & laser coupling  
• Thermal management & manufacturing complexity extremely high  
• Represents highest entry barrier in semiconductor value chain  

AI STACK EVOLUTION  
• Traditional: Compute (chips) driven  
• Emerging:  
  Power → Data Center → Servers → GPUs →  
  Packaging → Photonics Packaging → AI Models  
• Packaging now core performance layer, not backend  

INDIA OPPORTUNITY  
• Realistic entry point: Semiconductor packaging (not advanced fabs)  
• Direct players: Tata Electronics, CG Power, SPEL Semiconductor  
• Ecosystem support: Kaynes Technology, Dixon Technologies  
• Niche exposure: izmo Ltd. (photonics-linked capabilities)  

STRATEGIC INSIGHT  
• Future winners will be companies enabling high-speed data movement  
• Packaging + interconnect innovation = next semiconductor cycle driver  

IMPACT  
• Positive (Long-term) – structural shift creates new high-value opportunity beyond traditional chip manufacturing

Foreigners are dumping Asian stocks at a record pace:

Investors from overseas have sold -$52 billion of Asian emerging-market equities excluding China so far in March, the biggest monthly withdrawal on record.

This exceeds the previous full-month record set during the 2020 pandemic by 49% and the Russia-Ukraine war June 2022 outflow by +148%.

The biggest withdrawals were posted in Taiwan, South Korea, and India, countries that are most dependent on oil imports.

This comes as Asian economies account for ~80% of crude demand flowing through the Strait of Hormuz.

Asia is taking the hardest hit from the Iran War.

HOLLOW CORE FIBER (HCF): NEXT-GEN AI INFRASTRUCTURE THEME

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• AI bottleneck shifting from compute → data movement  
• Traditional fiber: light through glass (higher latency, energy loss)  
• HCF: light through air → faster, efficient, scalable  

TECH ADVANTAGE (WHY HCF MATTERS)  
• ~30–50% lower latency  
• Near vacuum-speed data transmission  
• Lower power consumption (fewer repeaters)  
• Higher bandwidth with minimal signal distortion  

---

STRUCTURAL IMPACT ON AI ECOSYSTEM  

1. AI SCALING UNLOCK  
• Faster GPU-to-GPU communication across geographies  
• Enables distributed AI training clusters  

2. DATA CENTER SHIFT  
• DCs can move closer to cheap renewable power sources  
• Reduces dependency on urban locations  

3. ENERGY EFFICIENCY  
• Lower transmission losses → lower infra + energy cost  
• Critical as AI power demand explodes  

4. TELECOM CAPEX CYCLE  
• Potential multi-year upgrade cycle in fiber networks  
• Similar to 5G-led telecom capex wave  

---

INDIA OPPORTUNITY  
• Currently import-dependent in optical technologies  
• HCF creates fresh domestic manufacturing + R&D opportunity  
• Early-stage theme → long runway  

---

POTENTIAL BENEFICIARIES (INDIA)  

OPTICAL / TELECOM CORE PLAYERS  
• HFCL – Developing HCF tech with IIT Delhi  
• Sterlite Technologies – Global fiber leader  
• Tejas Networks – Optical networking & transmission  

ECOSYSTEM PLAYERS  
• Aksh Optifibre – Optical fiber niche player  
• Polycab India – Cables & last-mile infra  
• KEI Industries – Infra + transmission cables  

---

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• AI is evolving into a full-stack infra story:  
  Power → Data Centers → GPUs → Networks → Fiber  
• HCF sits at the network + data movement layer  
• Early adoption phase → high optionality, high uncertainty  

---

RISKS  
• Technology still evolving → commercialization timeline uncertain  
• High capex requirements for large-scale rollout  
• Competition from incremental improvements in existing fiber tech  

---

BIG PICTURE  
• Next decade winners may not just be chipmakers  
• Data movement enablers (fiber, networks) could become silent compounders  
• HCF = potential backbone of global AI infrastructure  

---

IMPACT  
• Positive (Long-Term Theme) – Early-stage but high-impact technology with potential to drive next telecom & AI infra capex cycle

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Balanced strategy: Renewable storage + thermal expansion

• JV structure supports shared capital burden and execution  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Strong capex pipeline enhances long-term growth visibility and strengthens position in both renewable storage and thermal generation

• Relief provides temporary regulatory comfort

IMPACT

GUJARAT AMBUJA EXPORTS: CAPACITY EXPANSION UPDATE

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• Commercial production begins at new Maltodextrin facility in Hubli, Karnataka  
• Capacity expanded from 7,000 MTPA to 23,000 MTPA  
• New plant operational from March 28, 2026  

GROWTH DRIVERS  
• Significant capacity addition in value-added product segment  
• Strengthens presence in food ingredients and starch derivatives  
• Supports increasing demand for Maltodextrin  

FINANCIAL IMPACT  
• Higher production capacity to drive revenue growth  
• Improved operating leverage with scale expansion  
• Enhances long-term earnings visibility

• Expands product portfolio and strengthens value-added mix  
• Positions company for growth in domestic and export markets

• Positive – Capacity expansion supports revenue growth, scale benefits, and strengthens product portfolio

UAE–US RELATIONS: STRATEGIC ALIGNMENT UPDATE

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• UAE reaffirms strong strategic partnership with the United States  
• Statement indicates intent to “double down” on bilateral ties  

GROWTH DRIVERS / IMPLICATIONS  
• Strengthening cooperation in defence, energy, and technology sectors  
• Increased geopolitical alignment amid global tensions  
• Potential boost to trade, investment, and strategic collaborations  

GLOBAL IMPACT  
• Reinforces US influence in Middle East region  
• May impact regional geopolitical dynamics and alliances

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• UAE positioning as a key US ally in West Asia  
• Likely continuation of economic and defence cooperation  
• Enhances long-term bilateral engagement  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Strengthened geopolitical partnership supports trade, security, and strategic cooperation

KAYNES TECHNOLOGY: SEBI SETTLEMENT UPDATE

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• Former MD Ramesh Kunhikannan settles SEBI show-cause notice  
• Matter related to alleged insider trading regulation violation  
• Settlement amount: ₹23.42 lakh (paid personally)  

FINANCIAL IMPACT  
• No impact on company financials  
• Penalty paid from personal account of former MD  

GROWTH DRIVERS / STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Issue pertains to individual, not company operations  
• Business fundamentals and execution remain unaffected  

REGULATORY VIEW  
• Matter resolved via SEBI settlement order  
• Removes regulatory overhang related to this issue  

IMPACT  
• Neutral – No operational or financial impact on company; minor governance sentiment impact

MOSCHIP TECHNOLOGIES: AMALGAMATION UPDATE

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• NCLT Hyderabad approves merger of Softnautics Pvt Ltd & Softnautics Inc with Moschip  
• Amalgamation effective from April 4, 2025  
• Consolidation of similar business operations  

GROWTH DRIVERS  
• Streamlining of group structure  
• Reduction in compliance and administrative costs  
• Integration of capabilities across entities  

FINANCIAL IMPACT  
• Improved operational efficiency and cost optimization  
• Potential enhancement in profitability and margins  
• Better resource allocation across business units  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Simplifies corporate structure and enhances scalability  
• Strengthens focus on core semiconductor and engineering services  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Structural consolidation improves efficiency, reduces costs, and supports long-term performance

• One-time gain likely to support profitability

• Strengthens balance sheet in near term

Jal Jeevan Mission has enabled massive scale-up. Last-mile challenges remain

India has pulled off a quiet transformation under the mission with tap water coverage surging from 16.7% (2019) to 81.8% (2026), with several states achieving 100% household connections. A huge infrastructure leap in just a few years.

But the challenge of reliable supply yet to be addressed. Even among “fully covered” states, functionality lags with some states well below 50%.

1 in 6 households (16.9%) report insufficient water quantity. Quality concerns are far lower (7.6% dissatisfied)

**MORGAN STANLEY ON INFOSYS: KEY INSIGHTS**

**KEY HIGHLIGHTS**  
• Maintains Equal Weight rating  
• Target price: ₹1,760 (unchanged)  
• Acquisitions: Optimum Healthcare IT and Stratus  

**GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Combined revenue impact of ~1.2% expected in FY27  
• Strengthens capabilities in healthcare IT and digital transformation  

**FINANCIAL IMPROVEMENT**  
• Deals expected to be neutral to slightly dilutive to earnings  
• Optimum deal size ~US$465 mn  
• Stratus deal size up to ~US$95 mn  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Expanding domain capabilities via targeted acquisitions  
• Focus on long-term capability building rather than immediate earnings accretion  

**IMPACT**  
• Neutral – Strategic acquisitions enhance capabilities but near-term earnings dilution limits upside

• Neutral – Strong fundamentals and synergies offset by limited valuation upside in near term

reports.text:
**HSBC INDIA EQ STRATEGY: KEY INSIGHTS**

**KEY HIGHLIGHTS**  
• notes market movement in-line with historical oil volatility phases  
• 20% rise in crude → ~1.5% cut in earnings  
• 10% supply-driven oil spike → ~1.3% decline in broader equity indices  

**GROWTH DRIVERS**  
• Preference for defensive sectors amid volatility  
• Opportunities emerging in stocks corrected due to recent weakness  
• Focus on structurally strong businesses with long-term growth visibility  

**FINANCIAL IMPACT**  
• March 2026 quarter earnings impact expected to be manageable  
• Inventory buffers likely to cushion near-term margin pressure  

**STRATEGIC VIEW**  
• Key ideas: Defensives, cross-fire impacted stocks, and structural winners  
• Oil-led volatility seen as cyclical rather than structural disruption  

**IMPACT**  
• Neutral to Positive – Short-term volatility from oil, but manageable earnings impact and selective opportunities for investors
